voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Argentina — open grasslands and scrublands of Patagonia
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its large eyes sit high on its head for a wide view.
Long ears pick up sounds across open, flat terrain.
Its nose detects the scent of its mate, pups, and nearby predators.
It spots movement far away in the open landscape.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in open grassland and scrubland in central and southern Argentina.
Open ground lets it see predators from a long distance.
It eats mainly grasses, which cover most of its home range.
It digs or uses burrows made by other animals for raising pups.
How does it send messages?: It bounds away in high leaps to signal speed and strength to a predator.
Males urine-mark their bonded female to signal ownership to rival males.
It grunts to signal distress when the pair bond is threatened.
Both males and females use anal-gland scent to mark the ground around their den.
What does it do when something changes?: It can run at very high speed in short bursts to escape predators.
The male moves in front of the female when a threat is detected.
The pair stays close together rather than separating during danger.
Pups crouch and hide near the burrow at the first adult alarm call.
How does it learn?: Pups follow parents on foraging trips to learn which plants to eat.
Young maras watch adults to learn how far to roam from the burrow.
Pups learn the alarm calls of adults during their first weeks of life.
How does it have babies?: Pairs bond for life and raise pups together in communal den areas.
Multiple pairs share large dens, but each female nurses only her own pups.
Pups can walk and run shortly after birth.
The male guards the den entrance while the female nurses inside.
What are its limits?: It cannot live in dense vegetation where it cannot see predators coming.
Loss of open grassland reduces the food and safety it needs.
Extreme drought dries up the grasses that make up most of its diet.
Hunting and habitat loss from farming have reduced its numbers greatly.
What does this teach us?: Living as a bonded pair in open land shapes every choice this animal makes.
The male watches for danger while the female feeds.
Open space looks empty to us but is full of signals to this animal.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Forms lifelong monogamous pair bonds — unusual among large rodents.
Lives in open grassland where long-distance sight lines are essential to survival.
Multiple pairs share communal den areas, but each female nurses only her own pups.
Locomotion is more like a small hoofed animal than a typical rodent.
still_debated: Why mara use settlement (communal dens) when pairs are otherwise independent.
How stable pair bonds are under drought or high-mortality years.
Whether urine-marking by males actually deters rivals or just signals identity.
common_failure_modes: Calling it a 'hare' — it is a caviid, not a lagomorph.
Describing it as a herd animal like capybaras — mara society is built around the pair.
Over-confidence in population stability; it is listed Near Threatened.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as nocturnal; peak activity is dawn and dusk in open daylight.
Do not mix male and female roles — male guards, female nurses.
Do not extend mara pair-bonding to rabbits or cavies.
